Math 401, Fall 2025: Thesis notes, S3, Coherent states and POVMs

This section should extends on the reading for

Holomorphic methods in analysis and mathematical physics

Bargmann space (original)

Also known as Segal-Bargmann space or Bargmann-Fock space.

It is the space of holomorphic functions that is square-integrable over the complex plane.

Section belows use Remarks on a Hilbert Space of Analytic Functions  as the reference.

A family of Hilbert spaces, Fn(n=1,2,3,⋯ )\mathfrak{F}_n(n=1,2,3,\cdots)Fn​(n=1,2,3,⋯), is defined as follows:

The element of Fn\mathfrak{F}_nFn​ are entire analytic functions in complex Euclidean space Cn\mathbb{C}^nCn. f:Cn→C∈Fnf:\mathbb{C}^n\to \mathbb{C}\in \mathfrak{F}_nf:Cn→C∈Fn​

Let f,g∈Fnf,g\in \mathfrak{F}_nf,g∈Fn​. The inner product is defined by

⟨f,g⟩=∫Cnf(z)‾g(z)dμn(z)\langle f,g\rangle=\int_{\mathbb{C}^n} \overline{f(z)}g(z) d\mu_n(z)⟨f,g⟩=∫Cn​f(z)​g(z)dμn​(z)

Let zk=xk+iykz_k=x_k+iy_kzk​=xk​+iyk​ be the complex coordinates of z∈Cnz\in \mathbb{C}^nz∈Cn.

The measure μn\mu_nμn​ is the defined by

dμn(z)=π−nexp⁡(−∑i=1n∣zi∣2)∏k=1ndxkdykd\mu_n(z)=\pi^{-n}\exp(-\sum_{i=1}^n |z_i|^2)\prod_{k=1}^n dx_k dy_kdμn​(z)=π−nexp(−i=1∑n​∣zi​∣2)k=1∏n​dxk​dyk​

Example

For n=2n=2n=2,

F2= space of entire analytic functions on C2→C\mathfrak{F}_2=\text{ space of entire analytic functions on } \mathbb{C}^2\to \mathbb{C}F2​= space of entire analytic functions on C2→C ⟨f,g⟩=∫C2f(z)‾g(z)dμ(z),z=(z1,z2)\langle f,g\rangle=\int_{\mathbb{C}^2} \overline{f(z)}g(z) d\mu(z),z=(z_1,z_2)⟨f,g⟩=∫C2​f(z)​g(z)dμ(z),z=(z1​,z2​) dμ2(z)=1π2exp⁡(−∣z∣2)dx1dy1dx2dy2d\mu_2(z)=\frac{1}{\pi^2}\exp(-|z|^2)dx_1 dy_1 dx_2 dy_2dμ2​(z)=π21​exp(−∣z∣2)dx1​dy1​dx2​dy2​

so that fff belongs to Fn\mathfrak{F}_nFn​ if and only if ⟨f,f⟩<∞\langle f,f\rangle<\infty⟨f,f⟩<∞.

This is absolutely terrible early texts, we will try to formulate it in a more modern way.

The section belows are from the lecture notes Holomorphic method in analysis and mathematical physics 

Complex function spaces

Holomorphic spaces

Let UUU be a non-empty open set in Cd\mathbb{C}^dCd. Let H(U)\mathcal{H}(U)H(U) be the space of holomorphic (or analytic) functions on UUU.

Let f∈H(U)f\in \mathcal{H}(U)f∈H(U), note that by definition of holomorphic on several complex variables, fff is continuous and holomorphic in each variable with the other variables fixed.

Let α\alphaα be a continuous, strictly positive function on UUU.

HL2(U,α)={F∈H(U):∫U∣F(z)∣2α(z)dμ(z)<∞},\mathcal{H}L^2(U,\alpha)=\left\{F\in \mathcal{H}(U): \int_U |F(z)|^2 \alpha(z) d\mu(z)<\infty\right\},HL2(U,α)={F∈H(U):∫U​∣F(z)∣2α(z)dμ(z)<∞},

where μ\muμ is the Lebesgue measure on Cd=R2d\mathbb{C}^d=\mathbb{R}^{2d}Cd=R2d.

Theorem of holomorphic spaces

  1. For all z∈Uz\in Uz∈U, there exists a constant czc_zcz​ such that ∣F(z)∣2≤cz∥F∥L2(U,α)2|F(z)|^2\le c_z \|F\|^2_{L^2(U,\alpha)}∣F(z)∣2≤cz​∥F∥L2(U,α)2​ for all F∈HL2(U,α)F\in \mathcal{H}L^2(U,\alpha)F∈HL2(U,α).
  2. HL2(U,α)\mathcal{H}L^2(U,\alpha)HL2(U,α) is a closed subspace of L2(U,α)L^2(U,\alpha)L2(U,α), and therefore a Hilbert space.

Proof

First we check part 1.

Let z=(z1,z2,⋯ ,zd)∈U,zk∈Cz=(z_1,z_2,\cdots,z_d)\in U, z_k\in \mathbb{C}z=(z1​,z2​,⋯,zd​)∈U,zk​∈C. Let Ps(z)P_s(z)Ps​(z) be the “polydisk”of radius sss centered at zzz defined as

Ps(z)={v∈Cd:∣vk−zk∣<s,k=1,2,⋯ ,d}P_s(z)=\{v\in \mathbb{C}^d: |v_k-z_k|<s, k=1,2,\cdots,d\}Ps​(z)={v∈Cd:∣vk​−zk​∣<s,k=1,2,⋯,d}

If z∈Uz\in Uz∈U, we cha choose sss small enough such that Ps(z)‾⊂U\overline{P_s(z)}\subset UPs​(z)​⊂U so that we can claim that F(z)=(πs2)−d∫Ps(z)F(v)dμ(v)F(z)=(\pi s^2)^{-d}\int_{P_s(z)}F(v)d\mu(v)F(z)=(πs2)−d∫Ps​(z)​F(v)dμ(v) is well-defined.

If d=1d=1d=1. Then by Taylor series at v=zv=zv=z, since FFF is analytic in UUU we have

F(v)=F(z)+∑k=1∞an(v−z)nF(v)=F(z)+\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}a_n(v-z)^nF(v)=F(z)+k=1∑∞​an​(v−z)n

Since the series converges uniformly to FFF on the compact set Ps(z)‾\overline{P_s(z)}Ps​(z)​, we can interchange the integral and the sum.

Using polar coordinates with origin at zzz, (v−z)n=rneinθ(v-z)^n=r^n e^{in\theta}(v−z)n=rneinθ where r=∣v−z∣,θ=arg⁡(v−z)r=|v-z|, \theta=\arg(v-z)r=∣v−z∣,θ=arg(v−z).

For n≥1n\geq 1n≥1, the integral over Ps(z)P_s(z)Ps​(z) (open disk) is zero (by Cauchy’s theorem).

So,

F(z)=(πs2)−1∫Ps(z)F(z)+∑k=1∞an(v−z)ndμ(v)=(πs2)−1F(z)+(πs2)−1∑k=1∞an∫Ps(z)rneinθdμ(v)=(πs2)−1F(z)\begin{aligned} F(z)&=(\pi s^2)^{-1}\int_{P_s(z)}F(z)+\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}a_n(v-z)^n d\mu(v)\\ &=(\pi s^2)^{-1}F(z)+(\pi s^2)^{-1}\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}a_n\int_{P_s(z)}r^n e^{in\theta} d\mu(v)\\ &=(\pi s^2)^{-1}F(z) \end{aligned}F(z)​=(πs2)−1∫Ps​(z)​F(z)+k=1∑∞​an​(v−z)ndμ(v)=(πs2)−1F(z)+(πs2)−1k=1∑∞​an​∫Ps​(z)​rneinθdμ(v)=(πs2)−1F(z)​

For d>1d>1d>1, we can use the same argument to show that

Let IPs(z)(v)={1v∈Ps(z)0v∉Ps(z)\mathbb{I}_{P_s(z)}(v)=\begin{cases}1 & v\in P_s(z) \\0 & v\notin P_s(z)\end{cases}IPs​(z)​(v)={10​v∈Ps​(z)v∈/Ps​(z)​ be the indicator function of Ps(z)P_s(z)Ps​(z).

F(z)=(πs2)−d∫UIPs(z)(v)1α(v)F(v)α(v)dμ(v)=(πs2)−d⟨IPs(z)1α,F⟩L2(U,α)\begin{aligned} F(z)&=(\pi s^2)^{-d}\int_{U}\mathbb{I}_{P_s(z)}(v)\frac{1}{\alpha(v)}F(v)\alpha(v) d\mu(v)\\ &=(\pi s^2)^{-d}\langle \mathbb{I}_{P_s(z)}\frac{1}{\alpha},F\rangle_{L^2(U,\alpha)} \end{aligned}F(z)​=(πs2)−d∫U​IPs​(z)​(v)α(v)1​F(v)α(v)dμ(v)=(πs2)−d⟨IPs​(z)​α1​,F⟩L2(U,α)​​

By definition of inner product.

So ∥F(z)∥2≤(πs2)−2d∥IPs(z)1α∥L2(U,α)2∥F∥L2(U,α)2\|F(z)\|^2\leq (\pi s^2)^{-2d}\|\mathbb{I}_{P_s(z)}\frac{1}{\alpha}\|^2_{L^2(U,\alpha)} \|F\|^2_{L^2(U,\alpha)}∥F(z)∥2≤(πs2)−2d∥IPs​(z)​α1​∥L2(U,α)2​∥F∥L2(U,α)2​.

All the terms are bounded and finite.

For part 2, we need to show that ∀z∈U\forall z\in U∀z∈U, we can find a neighborhood VVV of zzz and a constant dzd_zdz​ such that

∣F(z)∣2≤dz∥F∥L2(U,α)2|F(z)|^2\leq d_z \|F\|^2_{L^2(U,\alpha)}∣F(z)∣2≤dz​∥F∥L2(U,α)2​

Suppose we have a sequence Fn∈HL2(U,α)F_n\in \mathcal{H}L^2(U,\alpha)Fn​∈HL2(U,α) such that Fn→FF_n\to FFn​→F, F∈L2(U,α)F\in L^2(U,\alpha)F∈L2(U,α).

Then FnF_nFn​ is a cauchy sequence in L2(U,α)L^2(U,\alpha)L2(U,α). So,

sup⁡v∈V∣Fn(v)−Fm(v)∣≤dz∥Fn−Fm∥L2(U,α)→0 as n,m→∞\sup_{v\in V}|F_n(v)-F_m(v)|\leq \sqrt{d_z}\|F_n-F_m\|_{L^2(U,\alpha)}\to 0\text{ as }n,m\to \inftyv∈Vsup​∣Fn​(v)−Fm​(v)∣≤dz​​∥Fn​−Fm​∥L2(U,α)​→0 as n,m→∞

So the sequence FmF_mFm​ converges locally uniformly to some limit function which must be FFF (Cd\mathbb{C}^dCd is Hausdorff, unique limit point).

Locally uniform limit of holomorphic functions is holomorphic. (Use Morera’s Theorem to show that the limit is still holomorphic in each variable.) So the limit function FFF is actually in HL2(U,α)\mathcal{H}L^2(U,\alpha)HL2(U,α), which shows that HL2(U,α)\mathcal{H}L^2(U,\alpha)HL2(U,α) is closed.

which shows that HL2(U,α)\mathcal{H}L^2(U,\alpha)HL2(U,α) is closed.

Tip

[1.] states that point-wise evaluation of FFF on UUU is continuous. That is, for each z∈Uz\in Uz∈U, the map φ:HL2(U,α)→C\varphi: \mathcal{H}L^2(U,\alpha)\to \mathbb{C}φ:HL2(U,α)→C that takes F∈HL2(U,α)F\in \mathcal{H}L^2(U,\alpha)F∈HL2(U,α) to F(z)F(z)F(z) is a continuous linear functional on HL2(U,α)\mathcal{H}L^2(U,\alpha)HL2(U,α). This is false for ordinary non-holomorphic functions, e.g. L2L^2L2 spaces.

Reproducing kernel

Let HL2(U,α)\mathcal{H}L^2(U,\alpha)HL2(U,α) be a holomorphic space. The reproducing kernel of HL2(U,α)\mathcal{H}L^2(U,\alpha)HL2(U,α) is a function K:U×U→CK:U\times U\to \mathbb{C}K:U×U→C, K(z,w),z,w∈UK(z,w),z,w\in UK(z,w),z,w∈U with the following properties:

  1. K(z,w)K(z,w)K(z,w) is holomorphic in zzz and anti-holomorphic in www.

    K(w,z)=K(z,w)‾K(w,z)=\overline{K(z,w)}K(w,z)=K(z,w)​

  2. For each fixed z∈Uz\in Uz∈U, K(z,w)K(z,w)K(z,w) is a square integrable dα(w)d\alpha(w)dα(w). For all F∈HL2(U,α)F\in \mathcal{H}L^2(U,\alpha)F∈HL2(U,α),

    F(z)=∫UK(z,w)F(w)α(w)dwF(z)=\int_U K(z,w)F(w) \alpha(w) dwF(z)=∫U​K(z,w)F(w)α(w)dw

  3. If F∈L2(U,α)F\in L^2(U,\alpha)F∈L2(U,α), let PFPFPF denote the orthogonal projection of FFF onto closed subspace HL2(U,α)\mathcal{H}L^2(U,\alpha)HL2(U,α). Then

    PF(z)=∫UK(z,w)F(w)α(w)dwPF(z)=\int_U K(z,w)F(w) \alpha(w) dwPF(z)=∫U​K(z,w)F(w)α(w)dw

  4. For all z,u∈Uz,u\in Uz,u∈U,

    ∫UK(z,w)K(w,u)α(w)dw=K(z,u)\int_U K(z,w)K(w,u) \alpha(w) dw=K(z,u)∫U​K(z,w)K(w,u)α(w)dw=K(z,u)

  5. For all z∈Uz\in Uz∈U,

    ∣F(z)∣2≤K(z,z)∥F∥L2(U,α)2|F(z)|^2\leq K(z,z) \|F\|^2_{L^2(U,\alpha)}∣F(z)∣2≤K(z,z)∥F∥L2(U,α)2​

Proof

For part 1, By Riesz Theorem, the linear functional evaluation at z∈Uz\in Uz∈U on HL2(U,α)\mathcal{H}L^2(U,\alpha)HL2(U,α) can be represented uniquely as inner product with some ϕz∈HL2(U,α)\phi_z\in \mathcal{H}L^2(U,\alpha)ϕz​∈HL2(U,α).

F(z)=⟨F,ϕz⟩L2(U,α)=∫UF(w)ϕz(w)‾α(w)dwF(z)=\langle F,\phi_z\rangle_{L^2(U,\alpha)}=\int_U F(w)\overline{\phi_z(w)} \alpha(w) dwF(z)=⟨F,ϕz​⟩L2(U,α)​=∫U​F(w)ϕz​(w)​α(w)dw

And assume part 2 is true, then we have

K(z,w)=ϕz(w)‾K(z,w)=\overline{\phi_z(w)}K(z,w)=ϕz​(w)​

So part 1 is true.

For part 2, we can use the same argument

ϕz(w)=⟨ϕz,ϕw⟩L2(U,α)=⟨ϕw,ϕz⟩L2(U,α)‾=ϕw(z)‾\phi_z(w)=\langle \phi_z,\phi_w\rangle_{L^2(U,\alpha)}=\overline{\langle \phi_w,\phi_z\rangle_{L^2(U,\alpha)}}=\overline{\phi_w(z)}ϕz​(w)=⟨ϕz​,ϕw​⟩L2(U,α)​=⟨ϕw​,ϕz​⟩L2(U,α)​​=ϕw​(z)​

… continue if needed.

Construction of reproducing kernel

Let {ej}\{e_j\}{ej​} be any orthonormal basis of HL2(U,α)\mathcal{H}L^2(U,\alpha)HL2(U,α). Then for all z,w∈Uz,w\in Uz,w∈U,

∑j=1∞∣ej(z)ej(w)‾∣<∞\sum_{j=1}^{\infty} |e_j(z)\overline{e_j(w)}|<\inftyj=1∑∞​∣ej​(z)ej​(w)​∣<∞

and

K(z,w)=∑j=1∞ej(z)ej(w)‾K(z,w)=\sum_{j=1}^{\infty} e_j(z)\overline{e_j(w)}K(z,w)=j=1∑∞​ej​(z)ej​(w)​

Bargmann space

The Bargmann spaces are the holomorphic spaces

HL2(Cd,μt)\mathcal{H}L^2(\mathbb{C}^d,\mu_t)HL2(Cd,μt​)

where

μt(z)=(πt)−dexp⁡(−∣z∣2/t)\mu_t(z)=(\pi t)^{-d}\exp(-|z|^2/t)μt​(z)=(πt)−dexp(−∣z∣2/t)

For this research, we can tentatively set t=1t=1t=1 and d=2d=2d=2 for simplicity so that you can continue to read the next section.

Reproducing kernel for Bargmann space

For all d≥1d\geq 1d≥1, the reproducing kernel of the space HL2(Cd,μt)\mathcal{H}L^2(\mathbb{C}^d,\mu_t)HL2(Cd,μt​) is given by

K(z,w)=exp⁡(z⋅w‾/t)K(z,w)=\exp(z\cdot \overline{w}/t)K(z,w)=exp(z⋅w/t)

where z⋅w‾=∑k=1dzkwk‾z\cdot \overline{w}=\sum_{k=1}^d z_k\overline{w_k}z⋅w=∑k=1d​zk​wk​​.

This gives the pointwise bounds

∣F(z)∣2≤exp⁡(∥z∥2/t)∥F∥L2(Cd,μt)2|F(z)|^2\leq \exp(\|z\|^2/t) \|F\|^2_{L^2(\mathbb{C}^d,\mu_t)}∣F(z)∣2≤exp(∥z∥2/t)∥F∥L2(Cd,μt​)2​

For all F∈HL2(Cd,μt)F\in \mathcal{H}L^2(\mathbb{C}^d,\mu_t)F∈HL2(Cd,μt​), and z∈Cdz\in \mathbb{C}^dz∈Cd.

Proofs are intentionally skipped, you can refer to the lecture notes for details.

Lie bracket of vector fields

Let X,YX,YX,Y be two vector fields on a smooth manifold MMM. The Lie bracket of XXX and YYY is an operator [X,Y]:C∞(M)→C∞(M)[X,Y]:C^\infty(M)\to C^\infty(M)[X,Y]:C∞(M)→C∞(M) defined by

[X,Y](f)=X(Y(f))−Y(X(f))[X,Y](f)=X(Y(f))-Y(X(f))[X,Y](f)=X(Y(f))−Y(X(f))

This operator is a vector field.
